title: Chapter 13: The Price of Kindness
paragraphs: Long before the skies began to rot and radiation ash covered the continents in a thick shroud, this world was already ill. Its disease was not magic or monsters, but a quiet hatred rooted in centuries. In this world, beauty and light belonged to the High Elves in their marble spires, the gold and warmth of smithies were for the dwarves, but to the goblins belonged only damp, cold mud.

The Rotten Hollow was not even a village. It was a festering wound on the body of the earth, dug into a steep slope of a ravine far from the trade tracts. Here, in cramped burrows intertwined with the roots of dead trees, huddled those whom the rest of the world considered a mistake of creation. It always smelled the same here—of rotting leaves, dampness, and hopelessness.
In one of these burrows, where the ceiling was a layer of compressed clay, Yashil was born.

He was five winters old when he first understood what death was. In the world of goblins, death was not accompanied by solemn burial songs or beautiful speeches about reuniting with ancestral spirits. It came quietly, like an old, uninvited guest who simply had nothing to offer in return.

The winter was harsh. The forests around froze until they rang, and the pitiful supplies of roots were coming to an end. Yashil's mother, a thin goblin woman with eyes in which any spark except the instinct to protect her child had long since gone out, was fading slowly. For weeks she chewed only tree bark, giving her son the last dry berries and crumbs of moldy bread found at the dumps of elven outposts.
That morning Yashil woke up to an unnatural, grating silence. Usually, his mother's breathing was heavy, with a slight wheeze. Now it was gone.

The little goblin sat up on the bedding of dry straw. In the dim light breaking through a crack in the ceiling, he saw her. She lay on her side, curled up to keep warm. In her stiff, calloused green hand was tightly gripped a tiny, shriveled tuber of sweet potato. She hadn't eaten it herself. She had saved it for him.

Yashil didn't scream. In the Rotten Hollow, children didn't cry—tears took away too much precious moisture and strength. He simply took the cold tuber from her hand, sat down next to her, and for a long time watched the dust motes dancing in the beam of light. It seemed to him that time in the burrow had stopped, turning into a thick, impenetrable resin.

A few hours later, old Griz—the elder of the Hollow—squeezed into the burrow. He was missing half an ear, and a white scar from a dwarven whip crossed his face. Griz looked at the mother's body, then at the silent child, and dryly spat on the clay floor.

"Your mother died because she was weak, Yashil," the elder croaked. There was no cruelty in his voice, only a statement of facts forged by a cruel world. "She forgot the main rule of our tribe. In this world, only the one who takes survives. The one who hides in the shadows and steals from the strong. Giving what is yours is the lot of fools and the dead."
Griz sighed heavily, picked up the light body of the goblin woman to take it to the common pit, and turned at the exit.
"Remember this, pup. The high peoples look at us like a disease. They will never extend a hand to you. If you are kind—they will crush you. Become a shadow. Become a rat. Rats at least survive."
But old Griz was wrong.

Left alone in the cold burrow, Yashil looked at the sweet potato in his palm. Then he turned his gaze to the place where his mother had just lain. In his childish mind, not yet poisoned by the century-old malice of his people, an alternative, frighteningly mature thought was forming.

His mother was not weak. The ability to give up one's own life for another is not cowardice. It required such a colossal, unfathomable internal strength that even the elven lords in their shimmering armor did not possess. If the world considers this weakness, then the world itself is ill.
On that day, Yashil made a decision that would forever change the history of the continent. He refused to hate.

Years passed. The little goblin grew, but remained an outcast even among his own. While other teenagers of the Rotten Hollow learned to cut purses from drunk mercenaries on the outskirts of the tracts or honed strikes with a sharpened rusty iron in the back, Yashil sat for hours on the high edge of the ravine.
From there, through the thick thickets of thorny bushes, the Great Road was visible, paved with white stone.

Yashil watched. He saw the processions of the High Elves passing by—beautiful, arrogant creatures whose clothes smelled of spring and magic. He saw the trade caravans of dwarves, jingling with gold and steel. And he saw how they treated the world.

Once he watched an elven rider lash a blind old half-orc with a whip for fun because he didn't clear the way in time. Another time he saw guards driving a beaten, hungry Hydroli from the city gates just because the color of his glow seemed "too dim and sickly" to them.

Whenever he saw this cruelty, Yashil felt not anger. He felt a deep, heart-wrenching melancholy. These creatures possessed magic, built palaces reaching for the clouds, but inside they were absolutely empty and frightened. They erected walls of racial purity and pride because they were panickly afraid to admit their vulnerability.

"If I answer them with a knife strike," Yashil said quietly one day, sitting on the edge of the ravine and watching the crimson sun set below the horizon, "I will only prove to them that they are right. I will become the monster they expect to see."
He lowered his gaze to his small, green hands. The hands of a creature destined from birth to pick at the dirt.
"Hating is too easy," the goblin whispered to himself. "Hating is the weapon of the weak. To change them, one must become that which breaks their logic."
